one_line_takeaway: The first dedicated survey of membership inference attacks on recommender systems, organizing attacks by user/interaction/social granularity and by target architecture, cataloging proactive and post-hoc defenses, and consolidating datasets/models/metrics — its value is the taxonomy and resource map, not any new experiment.
key_finding: The survey's synthesized takeaways: (1) RecSys MIAs succeed even in realistic black-box settings that see only ranked lists, and the field has moved from user-level to the harder interaction-level and social-level attacks and to LLM-based/federated targets; (2) the recurring exploitable signal is the embedding/similarity difference between a user's interacted items and the system's recommended items for members vs non-members, plus posterior-confidence separability where posteriors are available; (3) defenses are underdeveloped and all face a sharp privacy-utility trade-off — the survey highlights that differential privacy and regularization defenses buy privacy only at large utility cost; and (4) common assumptions in early attacks/defenses (e.g., that non-members receive globally popular recommendations) are brittle because modern personalized RecSys handle cold-start well.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an empirical paper): it presents what the authors claim is the first comprehensive survey dedicated to membership inference attacks (MIAs) on recommender systems (RecSys). Its own contributions are synthesis artifacts, not new attacks: (1) a novel taxonomy of RecSys MIAs organized along attack granularity (user-level, interaction-level, social-level), target-model type (matrix-factorization, sequential, graph-based, LLM-based, federated), adversarial knowledge (black-box vs white-box), and training regime (centralized vs federated); (2) a parallel taxonomy of defenses split into proactive (differential privacy, regularization, popularity randomization) and post-hoc (machine unlearning, privacy risk estimation); (3) a consolidated summary of evaluation resources — benchmark datasets, RecSys model architectures, and metrics — mapped to each design principle; and (4) an open-source living repository of the surveyed papers plus a discussion of open challenges and future directions. The authors explicitly position this against Hu et al. (2022), a general MIA survey that covered only one RecSys MIA paper.
threat_model: The surveyed threat is membership inference: given a trained target RecSys model f and a target record z, an adversary decides whether z was in f's training set, framed as a binary hypothesis test (H0: z not in D vs H1: z in D). The survey stresses features that distinguish RecSys MIAs from classification/LLM MIAs: attacks target three distinct levels (whether a USER was in training, whether a specific user-item INTERACTION was in training, and whether a SOCIAL link between two users exists); adversaries typically cannot see posterior probabilities and instead observe only ranked top-K recommendation lists; and attacks often need an extra item-embedding-generating (IEG) dataset disjoint from target and shadow data. Two knowledge dimensions are formalized — knowledge of the training-data distribution (enabling a shadow dataset) and knowledge of the target model (posteriors, hyperparameters such as negative-sampling ratio). Black-box (only distribution + interacted-item set + recommended-item set) is emphasized as the realistic setting; white-box (parameters, posteriors) is noted as mainly relevant to Federated RecSys where parameter updates leak to the server, or to developer-side privacy risk scoring.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new empirical evidence and inherits the reliability of the works it cites; several surveyed papers are only preprints (the authors acknowledge this and invite updates). Coverage of interaction-level and especially social-level attacks is thin because the primary literature itself is sparse (SMIA is essentially the sole social-level work; IFed-MIA is the sole Federated RecSys MIA). The survey notes practical caveats it cannot resolve — e.g., IFed-MIA assumes the adversary knows the negative-sampling ratio, which an external attacker typically cannot observe, and shadow-dataset / IEG-dataset assumptions may not always hold. Some model descriptions read as loosely sourced (e.g., ECFAT is described as 'commonly cited as'), and the template contains unfilled ACM boilerplate (placeholder CCS concepts/keywords, a broken Figure reference), suggesting a not-fully-polished manuscript version.

datasets_used: Ciao (7,375 users, 99,746 items, 278,483 interactions; includes social trust links)
Flickr (3,074,947 users, 41,278,715 items, 187,168,754 interactions; includes user-user social links)
quantitative_results: NOTE: this is a survey with NO original experiments; every number below is reported by the survey FROM the primary work it cites, not measured by the survey authors.
IFed-MIA (Yuan et al.): >90% accuracy in predicting user-item interactions on Fed-NCF and Fed-LightGCN across multiple datasets, using only public parameter updates.
Federated defense (Yuan et al.): LDP reduces attack effectiveness by up to 67% but decreases recommendation accuracy by 65%.
Centralized defense: reaching 40% defense effectiveness causes model performance to drop by 90%.
RGL regularization (Zhong et al., under MINER attack): increases defense efficiency by up to 37.8%, at a utility drop of up to 12.7%.
SAL / Socially Adversarial Learning (Zhao et al.): enhances defense efficiency by up to 6.0%, reducing model performance by as much as 9.7%.
Privacy risk score (He et al.): defined as ln(TPR/FPR) (equivalently epsilon_hat ~ log(TPR / max(FPR, eps_num))), derived from the (epsilon,delta)-DP bound TPR <= e^epsilon * FPR + delta.
baselines_compared: As a survey it compares attack families against each other rather than running baselines. Attack methods surveyed: Zhang et al. item-difference user-level MIA (ID-MIA); DL-MIA (Wang et al.); SF-MIA shadow-free (Chi et al.); ME-MIA model-extraction for sequential RecSys (Zhu et al.); MINER interaction-level KG-based (Zhong et al.); RecLiRA (He et al.); SMIA social-level (Zhao et al.); IFed-MIA federated white-box (Yuan et al.); He et al.'s five LLM/ICL attacks (Inquiry, Contrast, Semantic, Hallucination, Poisoning).

Target RecSys models cataloged for evaluation: ICF, LFM, NCF/NeuMF, LightGCN, GRU4Rec, BERT4Rec, STAMP, NARM, CKE, KGAT, ECFAT, DiffNet++, DESIGN, GDMSR; plus LLM-based frameworks P5, M6-Rec, TALLRec.

Defenses cataloged: differential privacy / LDP; regularization (RGL, SAL, federated update-control penalty); popularity randomization (Zhang et al.); machine unlearning (RecEraser, UltraRE, LASER; SISA-style exact and influence-function/fine-tuning approximate unlearning); privacy risk estimation (He et al.).
Evaluation metrics summarized: Attack Success Rate (ASR), ROC-AUC, F1-score, TPR/FPR (and TPR at low FPR), and Advantage = 2(1 - Accuracy).
